content.text: Emergence in language models refers to capabilities that appear abruptly at a certain scale — abilities that are effectively absent in smaller models and reliably present in larger ones, without those capabilities having been explicitly trained. Wei et al. (2022) systematically documented over 100 such emergent abilities across a range of benchmarks. The phenomenon is striking because it is not predicted by smooth extrapolation of smaller-model performance: below a threshold, accuracy is near-random; above it, accuracy jumps sharply. Understanding emergence matters because it determines what you can expect from a model of a given size — and where adding more compute stops helping.

A capability is emergent if it satisfies two conditions: (1) it is not present in smaller models at the same family, and (2) the transition is sharp rather than a smooth power-law improvement. Documented examples include:

To characterise emergence on a task, you evaluate the same model family at multiple scales and plot accuracy against model size (or compute). The signature of emergence is an inflection — a region where the curve transitions from flat (near-random) to rising sharply:

import numpy as np

# Hypothetical accuracy vs. model size data for a multi-step reasoning task
# (param counts in billions, accuracy in [0, 1])
model_sizes_b = [0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 7, 13, 30, 70, 130, 175]
accuracies    = [0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.09, 0.11, 0.15, 0.45, 0.72, 0.81, 0.85]


def detect_emergence_threshold(
    sizes: list[float],
    accs:  list[float],
    baseline: float = 0.20,     # random-ish performance below threshold
    jump:     float = 0.20,     # must exceed baseline + jump to count as emergent
) -> float | None:
    """
    Find the smallest model size at which accuracy first crosses (baseline + jump).
    Returns the size in billions, or None if no emergence is detected.
    """
    threshold = baseline + jump
    for size, acc in zip(sizes, accs):
        if acc >= threshold:
            return size
    return None


threshold = detect_emergence_threshold(model_sizes_b, accuracies)
print(f"Emergence detected at ~{threshold}B parameters")  # ~30B


def compute_sharpness(sizes, accs, window: int = 2) -> list[tuple]:
    """
    Estimate local slope of accuracy vs. log(size) over a sliding window.
    High slope regions indicate the emergence transition.
    """
    log_sizes = np.log10(sizes)
    slopes = []
    for i in range(len(sizes) - window):
        d_acc  = accs[i + window] - accs[i]
        d_log  = log_sizes[i + window] - log_sizes[i]
        slopes.append((sizes[i], d_acc / (d_log + 1e-9)))
    return slopes


for size, slope in compute_sharpness(model_sizes_b, accuracies):
    print(f"{size:6.1f}B params → slope {slope:.3f}")

The leading hypothesis is that emergent capabilities are multi-component: each component scales smoothly with model size, but the capability only manifests when all components are present simultaneously. This creates a threshold effect even when no single component has one. Consider multi-step arithmetic: the model needs to (1) parse the problem, (2) recall arithmetic facts, (3) apply them in sequence, (4) carry intermediate results. If each component is present with probability p(N) that grows with model size N, the joint probability p(N)^k rises steeply above a threshold where p(N) crosses a critical value.

For k = 5 components each at p = 0.8, joint success is 0.33. At p = 0.95, joint success is 0.77. A small improvement in each component produces a large improvement in the overall task — the curve looks discontinuous even though the underlying components scaled smoothly.

Schaeffer et al. (2023) argued that many observed emergence curves are artefacts of the choice of metric. Non-linear metrics (exact-match accuracy, pass@1 on code) amplify small continuous improvements into apparent phase transitions. When the same data is re-plotted with continuous metrics (token-level accuracy, Brier score, log-probability), the curve is smooth across scales.

def exact_match_accuracy(predictions: list[str], references: list[str]) -> float:
    """
    Binary exact-match accuracy: 1 if prediction == reference, else 0.
    Non-linear — small improvement in token probability can flip 0 → 1
    on a multi-step problem, producing a sharp-looking emergence curve.
    """
    return sum(p == r for p, r in zip(predictions, references)) / len(references)


def token_log_probability(log_probs_per_token: list[float]) -> float:
    """
    Mean token log-probability: a continuous, linear metric.
    When plotted against scale, this typically shows a smooth improvement
    rather than an apparent phase transition.
    """
    return sum(log_probs_per_token) / len(log_probs_per_token)


# Schaeffer et al.'s point: the same underlying improvement looks
# 'emergent' under exact_match_accuracy but smooth under token_log_probability.
# Neither view is wrong — they measure different things. The practical
# question is which metric matches your deployment criterion.

For tasks known to require multi-step reasoning (math, code, planning), small models may perform at chance regardless of prompting strategy. The threshold is around 7–13B parameters for most such tasks.
Evaluating only at one scale can be misleading. If your model is near the emergence threshold, a 2× scale increase may produce a large capability jump — or nothing, if you are still below it.
Emergent capabilities include undesired ones — deceptive reasoning, jailbreak susceptibility, persuasion. A model that appears safe at small scale may exhibit new failure modes after a scale jump.
For many reasoning tasks, CoT prompting lowers the effective emergence threshold — a 7B model with CoT can match a 70B model without it on some benchmarks.

Emergence is best understood not as magic but as a measurement phenomenon: tasks that require several sub-skills to all be correct simultaneously look discontinuous even when each sub-skill improves continuously. What changes at scale is not the kind of learning but the reliability of each component — and reliability compounds.
